scenario: shows all jurors when no filters are active
whyItMatters: Default state must display all jurors so admins see the full picture before applying any filter.
risk: Hidden jurors by default would make monitoring impossible.

scenario: keeps juror that passes a single group score filter
whyItMatters: Filtering by one group's score range must show only jurors whose score for that group is within range.
risk: Wrong filter logic could hide jurors who should be visible, or show ones who should be hidden.

scenario: hides juror failing one of two active filters (AND logic)
whyItMatters: When multiple group filters are active, a juror must pass ALL of them to remain visible — not just any one.
risk: OR logic instead of AND would show jurors who do not meet all criteria, defeating combined filtering.

scenario: does not apply a filter when min > max (invalid range)
whyItMatters: A filter where minimum exceeds maximum is logically impossible and should be silently ignored.
risk: Applying an impossible range would hide all jurors, completely breaking the view.

scenario: includes score=0 entry (boundary min value)
whyItMatters: A score of exactly zero must be treated as a valid intentional score and must pass a filter that includes 0.
risk: Excluding zero would hide jurors who intentionally gave zero points, silently corrupting visible data.

scenario: includes score=TOTAL_MAX entry (boundary max value)
whyItMatters: A score at the maximum possible value must also pass a filter that includes the maximum boundary.
risk: Off-by-one errors at the upper boundary could exclude perfect scores from filtered results.

scenario: excludes juror whose target group entry is unscored (null total)
whyItMatters: When a score filter is active, jurors who have not scored that group have nothing to compare and must be excluded.
risk: Including unscored entries could make an active filter appear to show incomplete data as if it matched.

scenario: first click on a column → sortMode=group, dir=desc
whyItMatters: First click on a column header should sort descending (highest first) — the most common admin need.
risk: Wrong initial sort direction would confuse admins looking for top or bottom scorers.

scenario: second click on the same column → dir flips to asc
whyItMatters: Second click should flip to ascending so admins can see the lowest scorers.
risk: Broken toggle would force page refresh to change sort direction.

scenario: third click on the same column → sort resets (sortMode=none)
whyItMatters: Third click should return to the original unsorted order so admins can see the default juror list.
risk: No reset path would permanently trap admins in a sorted state with no way back.

scenario: clicking a different column resets cycle to desc on new column
whyItMatters: Switching to a different column must start a fresh sort cycle on the new column.
risk: Inheriting the previous column's sort direction when switching would produce unexpected ordering.

scenario: desc sort orders higher scores first
whyItMatters: Descending sort must put the highest-scoring juror at the top of the list.
risk: Reversed sort order would completely mislead admins comparing juror performance.

scenario: asc sort orders lower scores first
whyItMatters: Ascending sort must put the lowest-scoring juror at the top.
risk: Reversed sort order would mislead admins looking for underperforming evaluations.

scenario: matches on juror name (case insensitive)
whyItMatters: Admins can quickly find a specific juror by typing part of their name, regardless of capitalisation.
risk: Case-sensitive search would require exact typing, making lookup error-prone.

scenario: matches on juror dept
whyItMatters: Admins can filter the grid to show only jurors from a specific department.
risk: Department search not working would force scrolling through all jurors manually.

scenario: OR logic — matches jurors whose name OR dept contains the query
whyItMatters: A search query should match jurors regardless of whether it hits the name or the department field.
risk: AND logic would only match jurors whose name AND department both contain the query, reducing results too aggressively.

scenario: no match → empty visible list
whyItMatters: A search with no matching jurors should show an empty list, not a crash or stale results.
risk: Showing stale results when no match is found would give admins false information.

scenario: builds lookup map and averages from completed jurors only
whyItMatters: The score grid average row must reflect only finalized jurors — not partially completed or editing ones.
risk: Including incomplete scores in averages would produce misleading published results.

scenario: excludes juror with editEnabled=true from average
whyItMatters: A juror currently re-editing must not count toward the published average while their data is in flux.
risk: Including editing jurors could push averages up or down while an evaluation is being revised.

scenario: includes juror with finalSubmitted=true and no finalSubmittedAt [Fix 2 regression]
whyItMatters: A juror marked final-submitted must be counted in averages even if the submission timestamp is missing.
risk: This regression previously caused valid submitted jurors to be silently dropped from averages.

scenario: returns null averages when all jurors are in editing mode
whyItMatters: When every juror is in edit mode, there is no finalized data and averages should show blank.
risk: Displaying stale or partial averages when everyone is editing would mislead the admin dashboard.

scenario: excludes partial-state entries from average (no total)
whyItMatters: An incomplete evaluation (some criteria filled, no final total) must not pollute published averages.
risk: Including partial scores would undercount the average and distort group rankings.

scenario: includes zero-score entry in average (total=0 is scored) [zero regression]
whyItMatters: A group intentionally scored 0 must be included in the average. Zero is a valid score.
risk: This regression caused zero scores to be treated as missing, inflating averages by silently dropping them.

scenario: computes correct numeric average across multiple jurors
whyItMatters: The arithmetic mean across multiple jurors must be calculated and rounded to two decimal places.
risk: Rounding errors in the average calculation would produce incorrect published group scores.

scenario: applies dense ranking with ties and excludes non-finalized groups
whyItMatters: Tied projects must share the same rank number. Unfinished projects must not appear in rankings.
risk: Incorrect ranking or showing incomplete projects would misrepresent results to faculty.

scenario: rankMap is stable when search filters the visible list [Fix 1 regression]
whyItMatters: Filtering the visible list must not recalculate ranks. A group ranked 4th must stay 4th even when viewed alone.
risk: This regression previously caused a searched group to appear as rank 1, which is factually wrong.

scenario: exports currently filtered/sorted list
whyItMatters: The Excel export button must export exactly the filtered and sorted list currently visible on screen.
risk: Exporting the unfiltered list would give admins and faculty incorrect export data.

scenario: filters rows by score status
whyItMatters: Admins need to isolate completed (scored) evaluations from in-progress ones in the detail table.
risk: Broken filter would force admins to scan all rows manually to find scored evaluations.

scenario: filters rows by juror status
whyItMatters: Admins need to see only rows matching a specific juror workflow state (e.g. Editing).
risk: Broken filter would make it impossible to monitor which jurors have active edit sessions.

scenario: filters rows by updated date range (from)
whyItMatters: Admins can narrow the detail table to evaluations updated after a certain date.
risk: Broken date filter would force manual scanning of all rows to find recent activity.

scenario: does not apply updated date filter when range is invalid
whyItMatters: When 'from' date is later than 'to', the filter must be rejected with an error and all rows kept visible.
risk: Applying an impossible date range would silently hide all rows with no explanation.

scenario: sorts newer year before older year
whyItMatters: Most recent semesters must appear at the top so admins do not have to scroll to find the current period.
risk: Reversed year order would bury the current semester at the bottom of a long list.

scenario: sorts Fall before Spring within the same year
whyItMatters: Within the same year, Fall semester is chronologically after Spring and should appear first.
risk: Wrong term ordering would cause admins to select the wrong semester when periods have similar names.

scenario: sorts Fall > Summer > Spring > Winter within the same year
whyItMatters: All four academic terms must be ordered correctly by academic calendar within the same year.
risk: Incorrect term ordering forces admins to hunt for the right semester, increasing risk of wrong selection.

scenario: puts semester with no year at the end
whyItMatters: Pilot or custom semesters without a year must not displace real semesters.
risk: Unnamed semesters appearing first would push current real semesters out of view.

scenario: calls onSetCurrent with the selected semester id when dropdown changes
whyItMatters: Changing the current semester dropdown must correctly trigger the backend update with the right semester ID.
risk: Sending the wrong ID would set the wrong semester as current, misrouting all ongoing evaluations.

scenario: disables delete button for the current semester
whyItMatters: The current semester cannot be deleted while evaluations are in progress.
risk: Deleting the current semester would destroy all ongoing evaluation data for the current period.

scenario: enables delete button for a non-current semester
whyItMatters: Non-current semesters can be deleted for cleanup — the delete button must be enabled for them.
risk: Blocking all deletes would prevent admins from cleaning up old semesters.

scenario: creates semester with valid input
whyItMatters: Admins must be able to create new semesters for each academic period.
risk: Broken creation would prevent starting new evaluation cycles.

scenario: calls delete callback for non-current semester
whyItMatters: Admins must be able to remove outdated semesters to keep the list manageable.
risk: Broken delete would leave orphaned semesters accumulating over time.

scenario: does not submit duplicate semester name
whyItMatters: Two semesters with the same name would cause confusion in the evaluation workflow.
risk: Duplicate semesters could cause jurors to submit scores to the wrong period.

scenario: shows create field error from API response
whyItMatters: If the server rejects a semester creation, the error must be displayed to the admin.
risk: Silent API errors would make admins think the semester was created when it was not.

scenario: shows error when file exceeds 2MB [Fix 6 regression]
whyItMatters: Files larger than 2MB are likely wrong files and must be rejected immediately.
risk: Accepting oversized files could hang the browser or upload corrupted data to the system.

scenario: shows error when required header columns are missing
whyItMatters: A CSV file without the required column headers cannot be correctly parsed and must be rejected.
risk: Importing a CSV with wrong headers would silently create jurors with no names or institutions.

scenario: shows error when juror_name is blank
whyItMatters: A juror without a name cannot be identified, contacted, or tracked in the system.
risk: Unnamed jurors would break the evaluation assignment workflow and produce unreadable reports.

scenario: shows error when juror_inst is blank
whyItMatters: Institution is required for juror identification and correct PIN tracking.
risk: Jurors without institutions cannot be uniquely identified when two jurors share a name.

scenario: shows error when same juror appears twice in the CSV
whyItMatters: Duplicate rows in a single file must be caught before import to prevent double-counting jury members.
risk: Silent duplicates would create two records for the same person, causing scoring anomalies.

scenario: treats existing system jurors as a warning, not an error
whyItMatters: Re-uploading a CSV that includes already-imported jurors should warn, not block — enabling safe incremental imports.
risk: Treating existing jurors as errors would prevent admins from using a master juror CSV file.

scenario: calls onResetPin with correct juror data when PIN button is clicked
whyItMatters: PIN reset must pass the correct juror object to the backend so the right account is updated.
risk: Sending the wrong juror ID would reset the wrong person's PIN, locking them out.

scenario: calls onDeleteJuror when delete button is clicked
whyItMatters: Admins must be able to remove incorrectly added jurors from the system.
risk: Broken delete would force database-level cleanup outside the admin panel.

scenario: calls onAddJuror with name and inst when create form is submitted
whyItMatters: The add juror form must submit both name and institution to create a complete juror record.
risk: Omitting either field at submission time would create incomplete records despite the UI showing them.

scenario: shows error when file exceeds 2MB [Fix 6 regression]
whyItMatters: Files larger than 2MB are almost certainly wrong files and must be blocked before parsing.
risk: Processing an oversized file could hang or corrupt the group list.

scenario: shows error when required header columns are missing
whyItMatters: A groups CSV without required headers cannot produce valid group records.
risk: Importing a malformed CSV would create groups with no number, title, or students.

scenario: shows error when group_no is zero
whyItMatters: Group numbers must be positive integers. Zero is not a valid group number.
risk: A group numbered 0 would be unidentifiable in the evaluation system.

scenario: shows error when group_no is negative
whyItMatters: Negative group numbers are not valid and would break sorting and display logic.
risk: Negative numbers would appear incorrectly in the UI and exports.

scenario: shows error when group_no is non-numeric
whyItMatters: Group numbers must be numeric — text values would break any comparison or sorting logic.
risk: Non-numeric group numbers could crash downstream logic that expects integers.

scenario: warns about invalid separator when students are comma-separated in CSV field
whyItMatters: Students must be separated by semicolons. Comma separation inside a CSV field corrupts the student list.
risk: Comma-separated students would be split incorrectly, creating partial names as student records.

scenario: shows error for duplicate group_no within the same CSV
whyItMatters: Two rows with the same group number in one file would create conflicting group records.
risk: Duplicate group numbers would cause evaluation scores to be assigned to the wrong project.

scenario: accepts valid semicolon-separated students (quoted CSV field)
whyItMatters: A correctly formatted CSV with semicolon-separated students inside a quoted field must import successfully.
risk: Rejecting valid files would force admins to manually enter each group one by one.

scenario: shows error for group_no exceeding 999 in CSV import
whyItMatters: Group numbers above 999 look broken in sorted list and indicate a data entry mistake.
risk: Extremely high group numbers sort incorrectly and can confuse admins managing many groups.

scenario: shows error when group_no exceeds 999 in add form
whyItMatters: Group numbers above 999 should be rejected at form level before reaching the server.
risk: Without a bound, typos like 10000 would create groups with nonsensical numbers.

scenario: edit modal stays open and shows in-modal error when save fails
whyItMatters: If the modal closes on failure, the admin loses their edit context and must reopen and retype changes.
risk: Data loss: admin retypes and possibly submits wrong data on retry.

scenario: edit save uses semesterId locked at modal open, not current viewed semester
whyItMatters: Changing the semester dropdown while edit modal is open could redirect the save to the wrong semester.
risk: Data integrity: a group's title/students saved into a different semester's record.

scenario: delete is blocked with a visible error when project id is missing
whyItMatters: Groups patched without a server-confirmed id should not trigger a delete RPC call with undefined.
risk: Silent server error or unexpected behavior when id is undefined.

scenario: import success message only appears after import resolves
whyItMatters: Showing success before the operation completes misleads the admin if the import subsequently fails.
risk: Admin may believe import succeeded and navigate away, only to find groups missing.

scenario: shows error when an empty CSV file is dropped
whyItMatters: Dropping an empty file produces no feedback and the admin doesn't know what went wrong.
risk: Admin is confused, may retry repeatedly or assume the feature is broken.

scenario: full project list refreshes from server after successful CSV import
whyItMatters: Without a full refresh, row-by-row patches may leave incomplete client state with missing server IDs.
risk: Subsequent edit/delete on imported groups may target undefined IDs.

scenario: calls onDeleteProject when delete is clicked
whyItMatters: Admins must be able to remove incorrectly imported groups from the system.
risk: Broken delete would leave wrong groups in the evaluation system, causing jury confusion.

scenario: edit save is blocked with conflict error when project updated_at changed while modal was open
whyItMatters: Saving stale form data over a freshly updated group would silently overwrite valid changes made by another admin session or realtime update.
risk: Data integrity loss during concurrent admin sessions on evaluation day.

scenario: edit modal closes automatically and shows error when the edited group is deleted externally
whyItMatters: If a project is deleted via Realtime while the admin has the edit modal open, saving would create a ghost or conflict.
risk: Silent stale-save on a deleted group could trigger server errors or ghost recreations.

scenario: create form shows no-semesters explanation when semesterOptions is empty
whyItMatters: Without a semester, group creation is impossible. The user needs a clear explanation instead of a silently disabled form.
risk: Confused admins waste time troubleshooting a disabled create button with no explanation.

scenario: Retry button appears in panel error and calls onRetry when clicked
whyItMatters: A transient network error should not require a full page reload. A retry button recovers without losing other panel state.
risk: Without retry, an admin must reload the entire settings page, risking loss of work in other panels.

scenario: shows Unlock Editing button for a completed, non-editing juror
whyItMatters: Only jurors who have fully submitted and are not currently editing can be re-opened for corrections.
risk: Showing the unlock button for ineligible jurors could open evaluations that are still in progress.

scenario: does not show Unlock Editing when evalLockActive is true
whyItMatters: The system-wide evaluation lock must take absolute priority — no individual unlock is possible while it is active.
risk: Allowing unlocks while the global lock is on would break the centralised control mechanism.

scenario: does not show Unlock Editing when juror has not submitted (not completed)
whyItMatters: Jurors who have not yet submitted cannot be re-opened for editing.
risk: Unlocking an unsubmitted juror would create an undefined workflow state.

scenario: checkbox is checked when evalLockActive=true
whyItMatters: The global evaluation lock checkbox must accurately reflect current system state.
risk: An unchecked checkbox when the lock is active would give admins false confidence that edits are allowed.

scenario: calls onRequestEvalLockChange(true) when unchecked checkbox is clicked
whyItMatters: Clicking an unchecked lock checkbox must request activation of the global evaluation lock.
risk: Calling with wrong argument (false instead of true) would toggle the lock in the wrong direction.

scenario: shows Lock Editing button when juror is in edit mode
whyItMatters: Admins must be able to see which jurors have open editing sessions and act on them.
risk: Missing button would leave editing sessions open indefinitely with no admin recourse.

scenario: calls onForceCloseEdit with jurorId when Lock Editing is clicked
whyItMatters: Force-closing an editing session must pass the correct juror ID to the backend.
risk: Wrong juror ID would close the wrong session, leaving the intended session open.

scenario: shows juror matching name substring
whyItMatters: Quick name lookup is essential when managing permissions for a large number of jurors.
risk: Broken search would force admins to scroll through the entire list to find one juror.

scenario: "lock editing" query matches only jurors in edit mode
whyItMatters: Typing 'lock editing' acts as a smart filter to show all jurors currently in an editing session.
risk: Broken semantic search would make it impossible to get a quick list of open editing sessions.

scenario: "unlock editing" query matches only completed jurors not in edit mode
whyItMatters: Typing 'unlock editing' shows only jurors eligible for re-opening — a quick action list.
risk: Wrong match logic could show ineligible jurors, leading to accidental permission changes.

scenario: shows error when new password is shorter than 10 characters
whyItMatters: Admin passwords must be at least 10 characters to meet minimum security policy.
risk: Short passwords are easily brute-forced, exposing all jury evaluation data.

scenario: shows error when new password has no uppercase letter
whyItMatters: Uppercase letters are required as part of the password complexity policy.
risk: Lowercase-only passwords reduce the character space and are easier to guess.

scenario: shows error when new password has no digit
whyItMatters: A digit is required in admin passwords per complexity policy.
risk: Passwords without digits are weaker against dictionary attacks.

scenario: shows error when new password has no symbol
whyItMatters: A special character is required in admin passwords per complexity policy.
risk: Passwords without symbols have significantly fewer possible combinations.

scenario: shows error when new and confirm passwords do not match
whyItMatters: The confirmation field prevents typos from locking the admin out of the system.
risk: A typo in the new password field with no confirmation check would set an unknown password.

scenario: calls onPasswordChanged after valid form submission
whyItMatters: A valid password change must call the completion callback with the new password so the session is updated.
risk: Not calling the callback would leave the session using the old password despite the change appearing successful.

scenario: renders placeholders and hides rings when there is no data
whyItMatters: The dashboard must degrade gracefully when no evaluations have started, showing a clear empty state.
risk: Crashes or broken UI on empty data would make the panel unusable at the start of a new semester.

scenario: clamps ring percentage to 100%
whyItMatters: Progress rings must never display more than 100% even if the data has an inconsistency.
risk: An overflowing ring graphic would break the visual layout and confuse stakeholders.

scenario: renders nothing when metrics is null
whyItMatters: The completion bar must not crash when no metrics data is provided.
risk: A crash here would break the entire Overview tab before any evaluations begin.

scenario: renders completion text with correct counts
whyItMatters: The completion bar must show the accurate number of completed vs total jurors.
risk: Wrong counts would give faculty an inaccurate picture of evaluation progress.

scenario: shows pending count when some jurors are incomplete
whyItMatters: The pending count helps admins quickly see how many jurors still need to finish.
risk: Missing pending count would require manual subtraction to track outstanding evaluations.

scenario: renders 100% completion without pending
whyItMatters: At full completion, the 'pending' label must not appear — all evaluations are done.
risk: Showing a phantom pending message at 100% would alarm faculty unnecessarily.

scenario: clamps completion % to 100 when completedJurors > totalJurors
whyItMatters: The progress bar fill must never exceed 100% width even with inconsistent data.
risk: An overflowing bar would break the layout and look broken to faculty.

scenario: renders without throwing when both props are empty arrays
whyItMatters: The juror activity list must render safely when no jurors or groups exist yet.
risk: A crash on empty data would break the Overview tab at the start of every new semester.

scenario: renders without throwing when props are omitted (uses defaults)
whyItMatters: The component must have safe defaults so it does not crash when a parent forgets to pass props.
risk: Missing prop defaults cause crashes that are hard to reproduce in development.

scenario: shows search input for filtering jurors
whyItMatters: The juror activity panel must include a search field so admins can quickly find a specific juror.
risk: Missing search input would force manual scanning of the full juror list.

scenario: renders without throwing with empty/null data
whyItMatters: The analytics tab must not crash when loaded with no data — it is shown immediately on navigation.
risk: A crash on empty data would make the Analytics tab permanently unavailable at semester start.

scenario: renders a loading indicator when loading=true
whyItMatters: When data is being fetched, the tab must display a loading state without crashing.
risk: A crash during loading would prevent admins from ever seeing analytics while data loads.

scenario: returns 'empty' for null or undefined entry
whyItMatters: A null or missing score entry must be identified as empty so the UI shows the correct placeholder.
risk: Misidentifying empty entries could cause wrong status labels and confuse jury workflow tracking.

scenario: returns 'empty' when no total and no criteria are set
whyItMatters: An entry where all fields are null must be classified as empty, not partial.
risk: Treating all-null as partial would miscount scored/unscored evaluations in progress reporting.

scenario: returns 'scored' when total is a positive number
whyItMatters: A positive total indicates a completed evaluation and must be shown as scored.
risk: Missing the scored state would prevent workflow completion detection from working.

story: Cell State Detection — Zero Score Regression
scenario: returns 'scored' when total is zero (zero is a valid intentional score)
whyItMatters: A score of zero is an intentional evaluation result and must be recognised as scored, not empty.
risk: This was a real regression that caused zero-score evaluations to appear incomplete, blocking workflow completion.

scenario: returns 'partial' when at least one criterion is filled but no total
whyItMatters: Partially filled evaluations must be shown as in-progress so jurors know they still need to complete.
risk: Showing partial entries as empty would hide work in progress from both jurors and admins.

scenario: returns 'partial' when multiple criteria are filled but no total
whyItMatters: Multiple criteria filled without a total still means the evaluation is not complete.
risk: Misidentifying multi-criterion partial entries could cause premature workflow advancement.

scenario: returns 0 for null or undefined entry
whyItMatters: A missing score entry must produce a running total of 0 without throwing an error.
risk: A crash on null input would break the live score display for any juror who has not started.

scenario: sums only numeric criteria values
whyItMatters: Only filled numeric criteria must count toward the running total — null fields must be skipped.
risk: Summing nulls or treating them as 0 would undercount the running total while scoring.

scenario: includes zero-value criteria in the sum
whyItMatters: A criteria value of 0 is explicitly set and must count toward the running total.
risk: Skipping zero values would produce an incorrect sum and mislead the juror about their progress.

scenario: returns full score when all criteria are filled
whyItMatters: When all criteria are entered, the partial total should equal the complete score total.
risk: A discrepancy between partial total and final total would confuse jurors about their score.

scenario: ignores string and null values
whyItMatters: Invalid or corrupt field values must be ignored rather than causing a crash.
risk: Summing strings would produce NaN, breaking the entire live score display.

scenario: returns 'editing' when editEnabled is true, regardless of other state
whyItMatters: Edit mode overrides all other states — a juror re-editing must always show as 'editing'.
risk: Showing 'completed' while a juror is actively editing would mislead the admin dashboard.

scenario: returns 'completed' when juror is in finalMap and not editing
whyItMatters: Final submission must be correctly detected to count the juror toward completion metrics.
risk: Miscounting completed jurors would give faculty wrong progress numbers.

scenario: returns 'ready_to_submit' when all groups are scored and not finalized
whyItMatters: When all groups are scored but the juror has not submitted, they must see the submit prompt.
risk: Missing 'ready_to_submit' state would leave jurors stranded without a clear call to action.

scenario: returns 'in_progress' when some groups are started but not all scored
whyItMatters: Partially completed evaluations must be tracked as in-progress to guide the juror.
risk: Showing in-progress as ready-to-submit would offer a premature submit button.

scenario: returns 'not_started' when no scores exist
whyItMatters: A juror who has not scored any group must be shown as not yet started.
risk: Incorrect initial state would give admins a wrong picture of who has begun scoring.

scenario: returns 'not_started' when group list is empty
whyItMatters: If no groups are defined yet, the juror cannot have started — must default to not_started.
risk: A crash or wrong state on empty group list would break the panel at semester setup time.

scenario: all jury step components render without throwing
whyItMatters: A crash on any step makes the entire evaluation day impossible for jurors.
risk: Undetected import or prop errors would surface only at evaluation time with no fallback.

scenario: shows remaining attempts on first failed PIN entry
whyItMatters: Jurors need immediate feedback on failed PINs to know how many tries remain before lockout.
risk: Missing attempt counter leaves jurors unaware they are about to be locked out.

scenario: lockout screen shown when error_code is locked
whyItMatters: After 3 failed attempts the UI must switch to the locked state to prevent further attempts.
risk: Failing to show the lockout screen would allow continued attempts and confuse the juror.

scenario: attempt counter decrements on each failed attempt
whyItMatters: Each failed attempt must reduce the displayed counter so jurors can track remaining tries accurately.
risk: A static counter would mislead jurors about how close they are to lockout.

scenario: score below 0 is clamped to 0 on blur
whyItMatters: Negative scores are invalid and must be corrected silently before writing to the database.
risk: Allowing negative values would corrupt the total score and ranking calculations.

scenario: score above max is clamped to max on blur
whyItMatters: Scores exceeding the criterion maximum inflate totals and must be rejected.
risk: Unclamped high scores would break rankings and violate rubric constraints.

scenario: fully-scored juror resuming session does not get auto-submit confirmation
whyItMatters: A juror who already scored all groups and returns to check progress must not be immediately forced into a submission dialog.
risk: Without the guard, every resume of a complete session would trigger an unexpected final-submission prompt.

scenario: SemesterStep auto-advances when only one semester is available
whyItMatters: Forcing jurors to manually select a semester when there is only one wastes time and adds unnecessary friction on evaluation day.
risk: Missing the auto-advance logic would leave jurors stuck on a selection screen with no obvious action.

scenario: juror chip shows Completed when allSubmitted is true
whyItMatters: Jurors who have already submitted must see a clear Completed status so they know their work is done.
risk: Wrong chip label could cause a juror to re-submit or be confused about their submission state.

scenario: juror chip shows In Progress when partial score data exists
whyItMatters: Returning jurors must immediately see their previous progress to decide whether to resume or start fresh.
risk: Displaying the wrong chip would mislead jurors about their current evaluation state.

scenario: EvalStep renders without throwing with minimal props
whyItMatters: A crash on the scoring screen would block all evaluations for all jurors.
risk: Any prop-shape change or missing dependency silently breaks the scoring UI.

scenario: Prev button is disabled when current group is the first
whyItMatters: Navigating before the first group would result in an out-of-bounds project index.
risk: An enabled Prev button on group 0 could cause the UI to render undefined project data.

scenario: Next button is disabled when current group is the last
whyItMatters: Navigating past the last group would result in an out-of-bounds project index.
risk: An enabled Next button on the final group could crash the scoring form.

scenario: Submit All button is shown when allComplete is true and editMode is false
whyItMatters: Jurors must be able to submit only after scoring all groups — early submission risks incomplete data.
risk: Button appearing prematurely could allow partial submissions; missing button blocks completion.

scenario: Score inputs are disabled and lock banner is shown when lockActive is true
whyItMatters: After the semester is locked, jurors must not be able to modify submitted scores.
risk: An enabled input in locked state could silently accept edits that are then rejected by the backend.

scenario: All scores saved banner is shown when groupSynced is true and editMode is false
whyItMatters: Jurors need visual confirmation that their scores were persisted before navigating away.
risk: Missing the banner could leave jurors uncertain whether their data was saved.

scenario: Thank You title includes the juror name after submission
whyItMatters: Personalised confirmation reassures the juror that their submission was accepted.
risk: A missing or broken title could be confused with an error state.

scenario: Edit mode title is shown when onEditScores prop is provided
whyItMatters: Jurors re-entering the scoring screen must immediately know they are in edit mode.
risk: Showing the Thank You title in edit mode could confuse the juror into thinking they already submitted.

scenario: Edit My Scores button is shown when onEditScores prop is provided and hidden otherwise
whyItMatters: Edit access is controlled by the admin — the button must only appear when the admin has enabled it.
risk: A visible edit button when editing is disabled could confuse or mislead jurors.

scenario: Total score is shown correctly for a fully scored project
whyItMatters: Jurors review their submitted totals on the done screen to verify accuracy.
risk: An incorrect total could erode trust in the system or cause disputes.

scenario: Start button is disabled when name or dept is empty
whyItMatters: Submitting without identity data would create anonymous juror records that can't be traced.
risk: An enabled button on empty input could create ghost juror rows in the database.

scenario: Start button is enabled when both name and dept have content
whyItMatters: Jurors must be able to proceed once they have entered valid identity information.
risk: A permanently disabled button would block all evaluations.

scenario: Error banner is shown when error prop is non-empty
whyItMatters: API errors (e.g. semester not active) must be communicated clearly so jurors know what to do.
risk: A silent failure would leave jurors stuck on the identity screen with no explanation.

scenario: Project count label shows singular form when activeProjectCount is 1
whyItMatters: Grammatically correct labels build trust and polish; plural/singular matters in professional contexts.
risk: Showing '1 Project Groups' looks broken and reduces confidence in the system.

scenario: Pressing Enter on the dept field triggers onStart when both fields are filled
whyItMatters: Keyboard-only users expect Enter to submit forms; missing this blocks accessibility.
risk: Jurors used to keyboard navigation would be unable to submit without a mouse.

story: aria-live announcement on save state change
scenario: SaveIndicator container has aria-live=polite and aria-atomic=true
whyItMatters: Screen readers must announce save state changes so jurors using assistive technology know whether their score was persisted.
risk: Without aria-live, a juror using a screen reader has no way to confirm their score was saved.

scenario: SaveIndicator shows 'Saving…' when saving and 'Saved' when saved
whyItMatters: The text content drives the screen reader announcement. If incorrect, the SR announcement will be wrong.
risk: Incorrect label causes misinformation for assistive technology users.

story: Retry button calls onRetry with correct pid
scenario: clicking the Retry button in the error banner calls onRetry with the group pid
whyItMatters: The retry action must target the specific group that failed to save, not a generic handler.
risk: Wrong pid would silently retry the wrong group or do nothing.

story: Error banner only shown on save error
scenario: error banner with Retry button renders only when saveStatus is 'error'
whyItMatters: The retry banner must not appear in normal (idle/saving/saved) states.
risk: A spurious retry banner would confuse jurors into thinking there is always a save failure.

story: DEV warning when VITE_RPC_SECRET is missing
scenario: console.warn is emitted in DEV mode when VITE_RPC_SECRET is not set
whyItMatters: Without the warning, a developer who forgets to configure .env.local will see opaque authorization errors with no clear cause.
risk: Silent misconfiguration wastes developer time during setup.

story: No DEV warning when VITE_RPC_SECRET is present
scenario: console.warn is NOT emitted when VITE_RPC_SECRET is set
whyItMatters: The warning must be conditional — it should not fire in a correctly configured environment.
risk: An unconditional warning would create noise and train developers to ignore console output.

story: isConnected guard prevents stale-closure DOM access after unmount
scenario: transitionend listener returns early without error when element is detached from DOM
whyItMatters: If the ScoreGrid unmounts during a momentum animation, the listener fires on a detached element. Without the guard, stale closures keep el and inner in memory and may access removed nodes.
risk: Memory leak and potential silent DOM errors on rapid navigation.

scenario: PinStep renders without errors when navigator.vibrate is undefined
whyItMatters: navigator.vibrate is undefined on Safari and some environments. The component must not throw when the API is absent.
risk: An uncaught error on vibrate would crash the PIN entry screen on Safari/iOS.

scenario: ErrorBoundary renders fallback UI when a child component throws during render
whyItMatters: Without an ErrorBoundary, any unexpected render error produces a blank white screen with no recovery path on poster day.
risk: A data format change from Supabase could crash the entire app silently.

story: Skip navigation link for keyboard users
scenario: skip link with href=#main-content exists in the DOM and is focusable
whyItMatters: Keyboard and screen reader users must be able to bypass the navigation bar and reach main content quickly.
risk: Without a skip link, keyboard users must Tab through all nav elements on every page load.

scenario: breakdown lines with count=0 are omitted from completedMetaLines
whyItMatters: Showing '0 in progress' or '0 not started' adds noise; only non-zero counts should appear.
risk: UI clutter that misleads admins about juror status.

scenario: scoredSub total label appears only when scoredEvaluations < totalEvaluations
whyItMatters: The sub-label '12 total' only makes sense when some evaluations are still unscored; it is redundant when all are scored.
risk: Always showing the total would confuse admins into thinking scoring is incomplete.

scenario: when totalJurors=0 and totalGroups=0, renders the 'Go to Settings' empty-state prompt
whyItMatters: New admins with no data configured need a clear call-to-action to get started.
risk: Without the empty state, a blank dashboard looks broken.

scenario: empty inputs produce empty arrays (no stale jurors, no incomplete projects)
whyItMatters: The selector must handle null/undefined inputs gracefully without crashing.
risk: Unsafe array access could cause runtime errors when data is missing.

scenario: jurors with progress=0 or status='not_started' are included in staleJurors
whyItMatters: Admins must see which jurors haven't started evaluating so they can follow up.
risk: Missing not-started jurors from the report makes it impossible to identify evaluations that need a reminder.

scenario: jurors with progress > 0 are excluded from staleJurors
whyItMatters: Only jurors with zero progress should be flagged; those in-progress or completed should not clutter the list.
risk: Including all jurors would make the Needs Attention card useless.

scenario: projects where completedEvals < totalJurors are included in incompleteProjects
whyItMatters: Admins need to see which projects don't have full juror coverage to identify evaluation gaps.
risk: Missing incomplete projects would hide projects that need more evaluations.

story: withRetry retries TypeError (network failure) with exponential backoff
scenario: a function throwing TypeError is retried up to maxAttempts times, then the error is re-thrown
whyItMatters: Transient network drops during scoring should not immediately surface as errors; a brief retry gives the connection time to recover.
risk: Without retry, a 500ms network glitch causes a visible save-failure error for the juror.

scenario: a function throwing an AbortError propagates immediately without any retry
whyItMatters: AbortErrors are intentional cancellations (AbortController); retrying them would ignore the caller's intent and waste resources.
risk: Retrying aborted requests could cause duplicate writes or stale data on semester change.

story: withRetry does not retry non-TypeError errors
scenario: a function throwing a non-TypeError (e.g. a Supabase business error) propagates immediately
whyItMatters: Business errors (RLS violation, wrong password, locked semester) must surface immediately — retrying them would be wrong and could burn PIN rate-limit attempts.
risk: Retrying auth errors would expose users to rate-limit lockouts.

story: computeOverviewMetrics returns all zeros for empty input
scenario: when rawScores=[], assignedJurors=[], totalProjects=0, all returned counts are 0
whyItMatters: The initial state before any data loads must not produce undefined or NaN values that could crash downstream rendering.
risk: NaN in totalEvaluations would cause StatCard to display NaN instead of '—'.

scenario: given a mix of completed, in-progress, editing, and not-started jurors, counts each category correctly
whyItMatters: The overview dashboard is the admin's primary tool for monitoring poster-day progress; incorrect counts would hide problems.
risk: A miscategorized juror (e.g., editing counted as completed) would undercount issues visible to the admin.

story: computeOverviewMetrics emptyEvaluations has a floor of 0
scenario: when scoredEvaluations + partialEvaluations > totalEvaluations (data inconsistency), emptyEvaluations is clamped to 0
whyItMatters: A negative emptyEvaluations count would display as a negative number in the UI, which is confusing and invalid.
risk: Negative counts in StatCard would confuse admins into thinking there is a data error.

story: PIN Reset Confirmation Shows Juror Name
scenario: confirmation step renders juror name when pinResetTarget is provided
whyItMatters: Admins must be able to see which juror they are resetting before confirming — wrong-juror resets are irreversible.
risk: Missing juror name would make it impossible to verify the right juror is selected before confirming.

story: PIN Reset Confirmation Shows Semester Context
scenario: confirmation step renders the viewSemesterLabel so admin knows which semester the reset applies to
whyItMatters: Resetting a PIN in the wrong semester would confuse the juror during evaluation day.
risk: Without semester context, an admin could reset a PIN believing they are targeting semester A while the view is scoped to semester B.

scenario: Cancel and Reset PIN buttons are both rendered in the confirmation step
whyItMatters: If Cancel is missing, admins cannot back out of an accidental reset trigger.
risk: A broken Cancel button forces admins to either close the modal with the X or reset unintentionally.

story: PIN Reset Result Shows New PIN
scenario: result step displays the 4-digit PIN from resetPinInfo.pin_plain_once
whyItMatters: The admin must see the generated PIN to share it securely with the juror.
risk: A missing or garbled PIN display forces the admin to contact the dev team instead of proceeding.

story: PIN Reset Result Shows Juror Name for Context
scenario: result step renders the juror name so admin knows whose PIN was just generated
whyItMatters: When multiple resets happen in sequence, the admin needs to confirm they are looking at the right juror's new PIN before sharing it.
risk: Sharing the wrong juror's PIN because the result step lacked context would block the wrong juror.

story: CSV Import Summary Shows Added and Skipped Counts
scenario: after a successful import, a summary message shows how many groups were added and how many were skipped
whyItMatters: Admins need to know whether any groups from the CSV were silently skipped due to duplicate group numbers.
risk: Without a summary, an admin would not notice that half the import was skipped, leading to missing groups on evaluation day.

story: Reduced Motion Preference Opens Data Table by Default
scenario: when prefers-reduced-motion is active, the ChartDataTable section is open by default instead of requiring user expansion
whyItMatters: Users with reduced-motion enabled cannot benefit from animated chart transitions; the data table provides an accessible alternative.
risk: If data table stays closed under reduced-motion, users who cannot use animated charts have no accessible fallback.

scenario: the score matrix table element carries role='grid' so screen readers announce it as an interactive grid
whyItMatters: Without role=grid, screen readers treat the table as a static data table and navigation shortcuts expected by keyboard users do not work.
risk: Regression during a refactor could silently drop the role attribute, breaking screen-reader grid navigation.

scenario: each juror name cell in the score matrix carries role='rowheader' so screen readers associate scores in that row with the juror
whyItMatters: role=rowheader is required for screen readers to announce 'Alice: Group 1 — 85' instead of just '85', providing essential context.
risk: Dropping rowheader roles makes the grid unnavigable for screen-reader users who rely on row/column header associations.

story: Submit Button Hidden When Evaluation Incomplete
scenario: Submit All Evaluations button is not rendered when allComplete is false
whyItMatters: Premature submission before all groups are scored would leave some groups with no evaluation data.
risk: If Submit is visible before completion, a juror could accidentally submit partial scores.

story: Group Synced Banner Hidden in Edit Mode
scenario: the 'all scores saved' banner is not shown when editMode is true
whyItMatters: In edit mode, scores are being changed; showing the synced banner would falsely imply the in-progress edits are already saved.
risk: A juror could trust the synced banner and navigate away without triggering the final save of their edits.

story: Submit Blocked Until All Criteria Filled
scenario: auto-done does not fire when any group has unfilled criteria
whyItMatters: Preventing premature submission stops jurors from accidentally submitting incomplete evaluations.
risk: Auto-submitting with missing scores would corrupt the final evaluation data.

scenario: moves to confirmingSubmit=true when all groups are synced
whyItMatters: The confirmation gate must appear before finalizeJurorSubmission is called.
risk: Skipping confirmation would auto-finalize without user intent.

scenario: handleEditScores populates scores, comments, and groupSynced from saved state
whyItMatters: Jurors editing existing scores must see their previous entries, not empty fields.
risk: Empty fields in edit mode would cause jurors to think data was lost.

story: Group Navigation Does Not Bleed Local State
scenario: scores for the previous group are unchanged after navigating to a new group
whyItMatters: Navigating between groups must not mutate scores of groups not being edited.
risk: Score bleeding across groups would silently corrupt other groups evaluations.

story: Save Called With Correct Payload on Blur
scenario: handleScoreBlur triggers upsertScore with correct semesterId, projectId, jurorId, and scores
whyItMatters: Wrong payload on blur would silently save incorrect data to the database.
risk: Misrouted scores would not be discovered until export or final review.

scenario: saveStatus becomes 'error' when upsertScore rejects
whyItMatters: Users must know when a save fails so they can retry or not navigate away.
risk: Silent failure would cause data loss the user is unaware of.

story: Retry After Failure Clears Error State
scenario: saveStatus returns to 'saved' after a successful write following a failed one
whyItMatters: After a transient error is resolved, the UI must reflect the recovered state.
risk: Stale error banner after successful retry would mislead the user.

story: Last Value Preserved Under Rapid Changes
scenario: only the last value is written when score changes occur before blur
whyItMatters: Rapid input changes must not cause race conditions that write stale values.
risk: An intermediate value winning would silently corrupt the saved score.

scenario: writeGroup returns false immediately when editLockActive is true
whyItMatters: Lock must prevent any writes even if the juror tries to blur or navigate.
risk: Writes bypassing the lock would allow scoring after the evaluation period ends.

story: Server-Side Lock Error Activates Client Lock
scenario: editLockActive is set to true when upsertScore error contains 'semester_locked'
whyItMatters: Server-enforced lock must be reflected in client state to prevent retry loops.
risk: Uncaught server lock would allow repeated failed write attempts.

scenario: step transitions back to eval with editLockActive=false when edit_allowed becomes true
whyItMatters: Admin unlocking a juror must allow that juror to resume scoring.
risk: Stale lock state would leave the juror unable to edit even after admin unlocks.

story: Lock State Cleared on Semester Change
scenario: editLockActive resets to false when a new semester is loaded
whyItMatters: Lock state from one semester must not bleed into another semester evaluation.
risk: Stale lock would lock jurors out of a new semester they are entitled to score.

story: Only Finalized Evaluations Counted in Rankings
scenario: project without any finalized juror score is excluded from ranked list
whyItMatters: Rankings must reflect completed evaluations only — in-progress scores would distort the ranking.
risk: Including partial scores would change ranking positions unfairly.

story: Tied Projects Get Same Rank With Dense Numbering
scenario: two projects with equal totalAvg both display rank 1, and the next project displays rank 3 (competition ranking)
whyItMatters: Competition ranking (1,1,3) is the correct contract — ranks after a tie must skip, not be dense (1,1,2).
risk: Dense-ranking display would show wrong rank numbers to the academic committee.

story: Rank Numbers Stable Under Sort and Search
scenario: filtering by group name does not renumber the visible project rank
whyItMatters: Rank is absolute; it must not recalculate based on what is currently visible.
risk: Rank reassignment on filter would show wrong positions to the admin.

scenario: project with mixed finalized and non-finalized jurors uses only finalized scores in average
whyItMatters: Including in-progress scores in the average would distort final ranking.
risk: Averaging non-finalized scores could shift rankings before evaluation is complete.

story: Juror Status Filter Consistent With Workflow State
scenario: filtering by 'Completed' shows only jurors whose workflow state is 'completed'
whyItMatters: Status label in the filter must match what the workflow state engine computes.
risk: Label/state mismatch would show wrong jurors under each filter option.

story: Score Status Filter Shows Correct Entries
scenario: filtering by 'Scored' in ScoreDetails shows only rows where all criteria are filled
whyItMatters: Score status filter must agree with the cell state engine to show accurate data.
risk: Mismatched filter logic would show wrong entries, causing admin to misread evaluation completeness.

story: Group Filter Shows Only Matching Rows
scenario: selecting a group filter in ScoreDetails shows only rows matching that group number
whyItMatters: Group-level filtering must be deterministic and consistent.
risk: Inconsistent group filtering would produce contradictory data.

story: Updated At Range Filter Shows Only In-Range Rows
scenario: rows outside the from-to date range are hidden when both bounds are set
whyItMatters: Date range filter is used to audit recent changes; missing rows would hide edits.
risk: Incorrect date filtering would cause admins to miss score edits made during the evaluation period.

story: Reset PIN Loading State Shows Feedback
scenario: Reset PIN button shows loading state while pinResetLoading is true
whyItMatters: Admin must see a loading indicator so they do not double-click and trigger multiple resets.
risk: No loading feedback could cause double-click triggering two PIN resets in rapid succession.

scenario: performing a second PIN reset displays the new PIN, not the PIN from the first reset
whyItMatters: Showing stale PIN from a previous reset would cause the admin to communicate the wrong PIN.
risk: Juror would be unable to log in with the displayed (wrong) PIN.

story: Dialog Reopen Does Not Carry Stale Juror Info
scenario: closing and reopening the dialog for a different juror shows the new juror name, not the previous one
whyItMatters: Targeting the wrong juror in the confirmation step is an administrative error.
risk: Resetting the wrong juror PIN would lock out a juror unexpectedly.

scenario: exportGridXLSX is called with the correct group column headers matching active groups
whyItMatters: Misnamed column headers would make the exported spreadsheet unreadable without cross-referencing.
risk: Wrong headers would require manual correction of every exported file.

story: Export Row Count Matches Filtered UI
scenario: exportGridXLSX receives only the jurors currently visible after filtering
whyItMatters: Exporting hidden jurors would produce a file inconsistent with what the admin is looking at.
risk: Admin exports data they believe is filtered but gets all jurors instead.

scenario: exportRankingsXLSX output has identical rank values for tied projects
whyItMatters: The exported ranking file is the official document submitted to the committee.
risk: Wrong ranks in the export would invalidate the official record.

story: Export Filename Includes Semester and Date
scenario: buildExportFilename returns a string matching vera_{type}_{semester}_{date}.xlsx pattern
whyItMatters: Filename must uniquely identify the export context to avoid confusion across semesters.
risk: Generic filenames would be overwritten, losing historical export records.

story: PinResetDialog Initial Focus Lands in Dialog
scenario: PinResetDialog has role=dialog and aria-modal=true for screen reader context
whyItMatters: role=dialog and aria-modal signal screen readers to confine their virtual cursor to the dialog.
risk: Missing ARIA attributes would allow screen readers to navigate behind the modal.

scenario: Cancel button in PinResetDialog calls onClose and has an accessible name
whyItMatters: Screen reader users must be able to dismiss the dialog via keyboard-accessible controls.
risk: Inaccessible close control would trap screen reader users in the dialog.

scenario: ScoreGrid sort header has aria-sort='ascending' or 'descending' matching current sort state
whyItMatters: Screen readers announce sort direction via aria-sort; without it, sort state is invisible to blind users.
risk: Blind users cannot determine current sort order or navigate sorted data meaningfully.

scenario: each rubric score input has an accessible name including the criterion label and group title
whyItMatters: Unlabelled inputs force screen reader users to guess what value they are entering.
risk: Users with visual impairments cannot reliably fill in correct criteria scores.

story: Sync and Error Banners Announced to Screen Readers
scenario: saveStatus error or lock banner has role='alert' or aria-live='polite'
whyItMatters: Dynamic status changes must be announced by screen readers so users know when saves fail.
risk: Silent save failures would go unnoticed by screen reader users, risking data loss.

scenario: juror fills identity form → enters PIN → selects semester → evaluation screen with score inputs is visible
whyItMatters: Happy-path regression test for the most critical user journey.
risk: Silent regression in any step would break the entire evaluation day.

story: Juror Sees Lock Banner When Semester Is Locked
scenario: juror login with locked semester shows disabled inputs and lock banner
whyItMatters: Lock enforcement must be visible at the UI level, not just enforced at API level.
risk: Juror would not understand why scoring is not responding.

scenario: admin opens Settings → Projects panel → Import CSV dialog opens → file selected → import completes
whyItMatters: CSV import is the primary data entry path before each poster day.
risk: Broken import on event day would require manual data entry for all groups.

scenario: admin login → Scores dropdown → Rankings option → rankings content visible
whyItMatters: Rankings view is the primary output of the evaluation.
risk: Admin unable to see rankings would require a workaround for result announcement.

scenario: admin on Rankings tab → clicks Excel button → file download starts with .xlsx extension
whyItMatters: Excel export is the official output submitted to the academic committee.
risk: Broken export on results day means no official document.

scenario: parseSearchDateParts('15 jan 2025') returns { day: 15, month: 1, year: 2025 }
whyItMatters: Audit log search by month name is the most common free-text query pattern.
risk: Broken parsing silently returns no results, making audit log search non-functional.

scenario: parseSearchDateParts('15.01.2025') returns { day: 15, month: 1, year: 2025 }
whyItMatters: European date format is the default notation for TEDU coordinators.
risk: Format rejection causes no results for the most common typed date query.

scenario: parseAuditDateString('2025-01-15T14:30') returns { ms: <timestamp>, isDateOnly: false }
whyItMatters: Audit filter date fields accept ISO datetime format; parsing must be exact.
risk: Wrong ms value shifts the filter window, returning wrong audit entries.

scenario: parseAuditDateString('1800-01-01') returns null
whyItMatters: Out-of-bounds years must be rejected to prevent RPC parameter errors.
risk: Invalid year passed to RPC could cause a 400 error or incorrect date range.

story: Date range error when start is after end
scenario: getAuditDateRangeError({ startDate: '2025-06-01', endDate: '2025-01-01' }) returns non-empty error string
whyItMatters: Invalid ranges must show an error before the RPC is called.
risk: Sending start > end to the RPC returns zero results with no feedback to the admin.

story: Build audit RPC params with date search
scenario: buildAuditParams(defaultFilters, 120, null, '15 jan') sets searchDay=15 searchMonth=1
whyItMatters: Server-side date search only works if searchDay/searchMonth fields are set correctly.
risk: Missing search fields cause full-scan fallback with incorrect results.

scenario: normalizeStudentNames('Alice ; Bob\nCarol') returns 'Alice; Bob; Carol'
whyItMatters: Student names pasted from spreadsheets use inconsistent separators.
risk: Unnormalized names cause duplicate juror records or failed project imports.

scenario: normalizeStudentNames deduplicates identical names (case-sensitive, preserves first occurrence)
whyItMatters: Duplicate names like 'Alice; Alice; Bob' are stored verbatim without deduplication.
risk: Duplicate names look incorrect in the UI and could confuse jurors during evaluation.

scenario: normalizeStudentNames deduplicates while preserving order of first occurrence
whyItMatters: Order of students should be preserved when removing duplicates.
risk: Reordering student names could cause confusion when cross-referencing other records.

scenario: validateRubric returns coverageError when first band min > 0 (gap at bottom)
whyItMatters: Score 0 would fall outside rubric bands if the bottom gap is not caught.
risk: Jury scores at 0 could be unclassified or cause incorrect MÜDEK calculations.

scenario: validateRubric returns coverageError when last band max < criterionMax (gap at top)
whyItMatters: Scores equal to criterionMax would fall outside rubric bands.
risk: Perfect scores would be unclassified in MÜDEK achievement reports.

scenario: validateRubric returns coverageError when adjacent bands have a gap between them
whyItMatters: A score in an uncovered gap would be unclassified.
risk: Internal score gaps break MÜDEK classification for those score values.

scenario: validateRubric returns no coverageError when rubric fully covers 0..max with no gaps
whyItMatters: Valid rubrics must pass so admins can save correct configurations.
risk: False positives would block saving valid rubrics.

scenario: validateRubric quotes both band labels in overlap messages
whyItMatters: Quoted labels make overlap errors easier to scan when band names contain generic words or spaces.
risk: Unclear overlap copy slows rubric correction.

scenario: validateRubric quotes the band label in reversed-range messages
whyItMatters: Quoted labels keep the error copy consistent with overlap messaging and easier to scan.
risk: Inconsistent band-name formatting makes rubric errors harder to read.

scenario: validateRubric returns coverageError when band count < 2
whyItMatters: A single-band rubric cannot meaningfully differentiate performance levels.
risk: Degenerate rubrics make MÜDEK achievement analysis meaningless.

scenario: validateRubric returns coverageError when band count > 6
whyItMatters: More than 6 bands exceed the intended rubric complexity and UI capacity.
risk: Excessive bands create confusing rubric displays and editor layout issues.

scenario: validateRubric returns bandLevelErrors when a band level name is empty
whyItMatters: Unnamed bands show as blank in rubric summaries and jury score breakdowns.
risk: Missing band names produce confusing 'Band N' fallback labels in results.

scenario: validateRubric returns bandDescErrors when a band description is empty
whyItMatters: Empty descriptions give jurors no guidance on performance expectations.
risk: Blank rubric descriptions reduce scoring consistency across jurors.

scenario: validateRubric returns bandLevelErrors for duplicate band names (case-insensitive)
whyItMatters: Two bands with the same name (e.g. 'Good' and 'good') are indistinguishable in reports.
risk: Duplicate band names corrupt rubric summary display and achievement classification.

scenario: validateCriterion returns max error when max = 0
whyItMatters: A criterion with max=0 makes all jury scores meaningless for that criterion.
risk: Zero-max criteria cause division-by-zero issues in percentage calculations.

scenario: validateCriterion returns shortLabel error when two criteria share the same normalized shortLabel
whyItMatters: ShortLabel is used as a display key in rubric summaries and score exports.
risk: Duplicate shortLabels cause column collisions in score grid and export files.

scenario: validateCriterion returns blurb required error when description is empty
whyItMatters: Criterion descriptions guide jurors during scoring.
risk: Missing descriptions leave jurors without context for what to evaluate.

scenario: validateCriterion returns mudek error when mudekTemplate is non-empty but no outcome is selected
whyItMatters: Criteria without MÜDEK mappings are excluded from outcome achievement reports.
risk: Unmapped criteria silently produce incomplete MÜDEK dashboard data.

scenario: validateSemesterCriteria returns null totalError when totalMax === 100
whyItMatters: Valid semester configurations must pass so admins can save correct setups.
risk: False positives would block saving valid semesters.

scenario: validateSemesterCriteria namespaces errors by criterion index (label_0, shortLabel_1, etc.)
whyItMatters: JSX error display looks up errors by namespaced key — mismatched keys silently hide errors.
risk: Wrong error key format causes validation errors to not appear in the UI.

scenario: isDisposableEmptyDraftCriterion returns true for a fully empty draft row
whyItMatters: Empty draft criteria must be stripped before save to keep the template clean.
risk: Saving empty draft criteria persists invalid data to the semester template.

story: Disposable empty draft criterion — max typed
scenario: isDisposableEmptyDraftCriterion returns false when max is '0' (typed zero, not empty string)
whyItMatters: A row with max='0' represents partial authoring — admin must fix or delete it manually.
risk: Auto-stripping partially authored rows would silently discard admin work.

story: Disposable empty draft criterion — rubric touched
scenario: isDisposableEmptyDraftCriterion returns false when _rubricTouched is true
whyItMatters: A row where the admin edited the rubric must not be auto-discarded.
risk: Auto-stripping rubric-touched rows would silently discard admin rubric work.

story: Position-based gradient for custom band names
scenario: getBandPositionStyle returns the red stop for rank 0 out of 4
whyItMatters: The lowest band must always map to the red end of the gradient regardless of band count.
risk: Wrong rank-to-color mapping would make lowest bands appear green, misleading all rubric viewers.

story: Position-based gradient for custom band names
scenario: getBandPositionStyle returns the green stop for rank 3 out of 4
whyItMatters: The highest band must always map to the green end of the gradient.
risk: Wrong mapping would show the top band in red, reversing the visual meaning of the rubric.

scenario: getBandPositionStyle returns green when total is 1 (single-band guard)
whyItMatters: A single-band rubric must not trigger division by zero and must still render colored.
risk: Unguarded division by zero would crash or render NaN-indexed color, breaking the pill display.

scenario: isKnownBandVariant returns true for all four canonical band names (case-insensitive) and false for custom names
whyItMatters: The known-variant guard must route canonical bands to CSS and all others to the gradient, never the reverse.
risk: A false negative for a canonical name would override the well-established CSS colors with gradient colors, altering the existing 4-band appearance.

scenario: shows default-template badge on semester list item when criteria_template is empty
whyItMatters: Admins need to know when a semester has no custom template saved so they can set one before scoring begins.
risk: Scoring against the wrong rubric due to silent fallback.

scenario: calls onUpdateSemester and the edit modal closes when API returns semester_template_locked_by_scores (no fieldErrors path)
whyItMatters: When the API returns a locked error without fieldErrors, the component must call onUpdateSemester exactly once with the correct payload and not crash.
risk: A regression in the save handler could swallow the API call or throw, leaving the admin with no feedback and inconsistent state.

scenario: onDeleteSemester is NOT called when the current semester's delete button is clicked (button is disabled)
whyItMatters: The current semester must never be deletable. Even if a click event fires on the disabled button, the callback must not execute.
risk: A regression removing the disabled guard or the onClick null-check could let an admin accidentally delete the current semester.

story: Realtime DELETE closes open edit modal and shows deletion banner
scenario: when a Realtime DELETE event arrives for the semester currently open in the edit modal, the modal auto-closes and a warning banner is shown
whyItMatters: If an admin has the edit modal open while another session deletes the semester, the modal must close to prevent saving data for a non-existent record.
risk: Without this guard, the admin could submit edits for a deleted semester, causing an API error or silent data loss.

scenario: adminGetScores maps raw DB row to UI ScoreRow with correct field names, status derivation, and timestamp normalization
whyItMatters: The score row mapper is the boundary between DB shape and UI shape. Incorrect mapping breaks all admin score displays.
risk: Refactoring adminApi without a baseline test could silently change field names or status logic.

scenario: adminListJurors maps raw DB juror row to UI JurorRow with correct field names and timestamp ms conversion
whyItMatters: Juror row mapping drives the juror management panel and grid displays.
risk: Incorrect timestamp conversion or boolean coercion breaks juror status displays.

scenario: adminProjectSummary maps raw DB summary row to UI shape with correct numeric coercion and avg mapping
whyItMatters: Project summary drives rankings and analytics tabs. Numeric coercion errors would corrupt averages.
risk: String-to-number coercion bugs would silently corrupt ranking displays.

scenario: adminGetScores derives correct status (not_started, in_progress, submitted, completed, editing) from score row fields
whyItMatters: Status derivation is a complex conditional that drives filtering, display, and workflow logic across admin tabs.
risk: Wrong status assignment breaks filter accuracy and overview metrics.

scenario: adminGetOutcomeTrends maps raw DB trend row to UI shape with correct criteriaAvgs and nEvals
whyItMatters: Trend data feeds the analytics chart. Incorrect mapping breaks semester comparison views.
risk: dbAvgScoresToUi integration errors would corrupt trend chart data.

scenario: getCellState correctly evaluates cell state using a non-default criteria array
whyItMatters: Dynamic criteria support is critical for semester-scoped rubrics. getCellState must respect the criteria parameter.
risk: Hardcoded CRITERIA references would break custom-rubric semesters.

scenario: getPartialTotal returns 0 when all criterion fields are null
whyItMatters: An all-null entry must produce 0, not NaN, to avoid corrupting average calculations.
risk: NaN propagation from null entries would break grid averages and export.

scenario: jurorStatusMeta has entries for all workflow states and cell states
whyItMatters: Missing status meta entries would cause undefined labels and icons in the UI.
risk: Adding a new status without updating meta would cause runtime errors in StatusBadge.

scenario: useScoreGridData builds lookup with criterion fields driven by criteriaTemplate parameter, not hardcoded CRITERIA
whyItMatters: Custom rubric semesters need the grid to index scores by their actual criteria, not the default set.
risk: Ignoring criteriaTemplate would show wrong scores for custom-rubric semesters.

scenario: jurorWorkflowMap correctly maps each juror to their workflow state based on scoring progress and edit flags
whyItMatters: The workflow map drives status badges, export rows, and grid row styling.
risk: Incorrect workflow state assignment would misrepresent juror progress to admins.

scenario: buildExportRows produces correct export data for jurors in scored, partial, and empty states
whyItMatters: XLSX export must accurately reflect each juror's scoring state, including partial totals.
risk: Export showing null instead of partial totals would mislead admins reviewing exported data.

scenario: ScoreDetails correctly filters rows by combined group number and status multi-select filters
whyItMatters: Combined filters must use AND logic — a row must match all active filters to be shown.
risk: OR logic or missing filter predicates would show irrelevant rows.

scenario: ScoreDetails correctly filters rows within updatedAt and finalSubmittedAt date ranges
whyItMatters: Date range filtering is critical for identifying evaluation activity within specific time windows.
risk: Timezone or end-of-day boundary errors would exclude valid rows.

scenario: ScoreDetails correctly counts the number of active filters across all columns
whyItMatters: The active filter count badge tells admins how many filters are applied, enabling quick filter awareness.
risk: Wrong count would confuse admins about whether filters are active.

scenario: App routes to jury_gate when a ?t= URL parameter is present
whyItMatters: QR code links contain a ?t= token. The app must immediately route to the gate page for verification.
risk: Missing URL param handling would break QR code entry for jurors.

scenario: App restores the last saved page from localStorage on mount, with correct jury access grant checks
whyItMatters: Page persistence ensures admins and jurors return to their last view on refresh.
risk: Incorrect restoration could expose the admin panel without authentication or skip jury gate.

scenario: App never saves jury_gate to localStorage, even when currently on that page
whyItMatters: jury_gate is a transient verification screen. Persisting it would trap users in an infinite verification loop.
risk: Persisting jury_gate would break page restoration for jurors.

story: migration SQL files use idempotent patterns
scenario: All migration files use CREATE OR REPLACE for functions and IF EXISTS for drops
whyItMatters: Migration files must be safe to re-run without errors or data loss.
risk: Non-idempotent statements would cause errors on re-deploy.

story: core schema migration uses idempotent patterns
scenario: 001_core_schema.sql uses IF NOT EXISTS and CREATE OR REPLACE
whyItMatters: Migration files must be safe to re-run.
risk: Non-idempotent migration would fail on re-apply.

scenario: 002_triggers.sql uses IF EXISTS for drops and CREATE OR REPLACE
whyItMatters: Migration files must be safe to re-run.
risk: Non-idempotent migration would fail on re-apply.

story: v2 API functions include tenant scoping
scenario: all v2 admin API functions pass tenant_id or semester_id for scoping
whyItMatters: Multi-tenant isolation depends on every admin API call being scoped to the correct tenant.
risk: Unscoped API calls would leak data across tenants.

story: Pending applicant cannot access admin panel
scenario: AuthProvider sets isPending=true when user has no approved memberships
whyItMatters: Pending applicants must not see any admin data before approval.
risk: Unapproved users accessing admin data is a security violation.

scenario: super-admin membership with tenant_id=null grants access to all tenants
whyItMatters: Super-admin must be able to manage all tenants without per-tenant membership rows.
risk: Super-admin being locked out of some tenants would block critical operations.

story: Jury flow has no tenant references
scenario: no jury-facing code imports or references tenant context
whyItMatters: Jury flow must remain tenant-implicit — jurors never see or choose a tenant.
risk: Tenant exposure in jury flow would confuse jurors and break the UX model.

story: Login form renders for unauthenticated admin page access
scenario: navigating to admin page without auth shows login form with Sign In button
whyItMatters: Admin panel must be gated by authentication — no data exposure to unauthenticated users.
risk: Admin data visible without authentication would be a critical security issue.

story: v2 settings API passes tenantId for scoping
scenario: adminGetSettings and adminSetSetting include tenant_id in RPC params
whyItMatters: Tenant-scoped settings must be correctly isolated per tenant.
risk: Missing tenant_id would cause cross-tenant settings leakage.

scenario: settings with tenant_id=NULL are accessible as global defaults
whyItMatters: Password hashes and system settings are global and must remain accessible.
risk: Breaking global settings would lock out all admins.

story: [Deprecated] stripSlugPrefix removes tenant code prefix from semester name
scenario: strips code prefix when present, returns name unchanged otherwise — no-op after Phase C.4 naming cleanup
whyItMatters: Kept as backward-safety test for the deprecated stripSlugPrefix helper. Semester names are now stored clean.
risk: Low — function is deprecated and no-ops on clean names.

story: computeTopProjects returns empty array when fewer than 5 projects
scenario: project list with <5 projects returns [] (minimum threshold not met)
whyItMatters: Rankings are meaningless with too few data points — minimum 5 projects prevents trivial rankings.
risk: Showing rankings for 1-2 projects would be misleading.

story: computeTopProjects returns top 3 by average score when >= 5 projects exist
scenario: 6 projects → returns top 3 sorted by totalAvg descending with rank added
whyItMatters: Top projects card must highlight the highest-performing projects for at-a-glance awareness.
risk: Wrong sort or limit would show incorrect rankings to admins.

story: computeTopProjects handles null avgScore values gracefully
scenario: projects with null totalAvg are excluded from ranking
whyItMatters: Projects with no completed evaluations have no average and should not skew rankings.
risk: Including null values could break the ranking sort or display.
